top_requirements.requirement: Explicit years-of-experience threshold in a customer-facing/implementation/consulting technical role — most commonly 3-5 years at "consultant"/"engineer" level, 5-8+ at senior/principal/architect level
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 148 of 202 postings state a numeric years requirement; 5 years is the single most common threshold (45 postings), then 3 years (33), 4 years (26), 8 years (19)

top_requirements.requirement: Hands-on integration/API work (REST APIs, data migration, custom scripting against a client's environment)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 119 of 202 postings reference integration work; 47 explicitly mention APIs, 24 REST specifically

top_requirements.requirement: SQL / relational database fluency as a baseline skill
Client-facing communication and presentation skills (workshops, demos, executive/stakeholder readouts)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 72 of 202 mention "communication skills" explicitly; 24 use the phrase "client-facing"; 18 mention presentation skills

top_requirements.requirement: Ability to manage multiple concurrent client engagements/projects in parallel
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 19 of 202 postings use explicit "multiple engagements/customers in parallel" or equivalent language (Okta, Samsara across 7 of its 9 postings, GitLab, Zuora, VML)

top_requirements.requirement: Product/domain certification or platform-specific credential (Salesforce, SAP, PMP, security clearance for federal roles, vendor product certs)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 81 of 202 postings mention certification of some kind; SAP-specific skill named in 23; Salesforce in 24

top_requirements.requirement: Willingness to travel to client sites, typically quantified as a percentage rather than described qualitatively
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 28 of 202 postings state an explicit travel percentage, ranging 10%-80% (most commonly 25-30%; federal/public-sector roles skew higher at 50-80%)

top_requirements.requirement: Cloud platform familiarity (AWS/Azure/GCP) as a supporting skill, not the archetype's core focus
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 41 mention AWS, 34 Azure, 17 GCP — usually listed as one of several acceptable platforms rather than a hard requirement

top_requirements.requirement: Bachelor's degree (or equivalent experience) in a technical field
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 22 of 202 postings explicitly state a bachelor's degree requirement (many others say "or equivalent experience")

top_requirements.requirement: PMP or formal project-management credential/skill, more common at strategic/enterprise implementation tiers
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 8 of 202 postings, concentrated in Samsara's "Strategic"/"Select" tier and Trace3/GDIT-style delivery roles

top_responsibilities: Running structured, milestone-based delivery engagements from kickoff/discovery through go-live and handoff — scoping requirements, configuring the product against the client's environment, and closing out with a defined end state (14 postings use "go-live" explicitly; ~20 reference "on-time/on-budget" delivery as a stated expectation).

Leading or co-leading technical discovery and architecture-review workshops with client stakeholders, often positioned as the "trusted advisor" or lead technical voice on the account (recurring across GitLab, Okta, Neo4j-style Technical Architect and Consulting Engineer postings).

Hands-on configuration, custom scripting, and system integration work — connecting the vendor's product to the client's existing stack via APIs, data migrations, and custom logic, frequently alongside debugging integration issues during rollout.

Delivering customer-facing training and enablement (classroom-style training, train-the-trainer sessions, delivery kits/training materials) as a standard deliverable, distinct from internal documentation.
Producing implementation documentation and delivery collateral (runbooks, configuration standards, case studies, internal delivery-kit contributions) as an explicit work product, not an afterthought.
Juggling multiple concurrent client engagements simultaneously and prioritizing across them — named explicitly at Okta, Samsara (7 of 9 postings), GitLab, Zuora, and VML.
Acting as a bridge across pre-sales, support, and product — feeding field learnings back to product/engineering and partnering with sales/solutions engineering during handoff into delivery.
At senior/principal tiers, mentoring or technically directing other consultants on an engagement and owning escalation/at-risk-account recovery, without a full formal people-management remit.

comp_signals.range_text: Enterprise Implementation Consultant — expected salary range $128,800-$181,930 USD depending on location (multiple postings, e.g. $145,544-$181,930 and $128,800-$161,000)
Implementation Engineer — annual base salary range $105,000-$276,000 across levels (e.g. $144,000-$240,000 base; Senior Implementation Engineer $184,000-$276,000)

Professional Services Technical Architect — US base salary range $123,840-$209,520; Senior Manager, PS Technical Architect $171,360-$288,660; incentive pay targeted up to 100% of base for eligible roles

Implementation/Enterprise/Strategic Implementation Consultant tiers — annual base salary ranges from $66,261-$153,409 depending on tier (e.g. base Implementation Consultant $66,261-$100,227; Strategic Implementation Consultant $100,406-$151,875; Enterprise Select $101,420-$153,409), plus performance bonus/variable pay and equity
Senior Implementation Consultant — national pay range $79,300-$95,000 USD ($87,700-$107,400 CAD for a Canada-based posting)
Customer Delivery Consulting Engineer - Security — starting salary range $134,300-$195,400
Sr Mainframe Technical Consultant — annual base salary range $100,000-$160,000
Senior Federal Technical Consultant (clearance preferred) — annual salary $105,500-$243,000 depending on state; Principal Federal HPC Technical Consultant $119,500-$275,000 in MD/Utah
Consulting Engineer, Manufacturing Systems Engineering — base pay range $167,000-$223,000 plus annual discretionary bonus
SAP-track Consultant roles (FICO/MM/PP) — base compensation ranges $110,000-$175,000 depending on specialization; Sr. Delivery Consultant $150,000-$175,000 (may go higher)
Delivery Engineer | Splunk — estimated hourly pay range $100-$125 (contractor-style); Sr. Platform Engineering Consultant — estimated pay range $190,000-$225,000
Implementation Engineer II, Fusus — base pay range $101,440-$169,344; Certinia Implementation Consultant — base pay range $101,085-$168,768
Implementation Consultant (North America - Ice Hockey) — base salary range $63,000-$106,000; German-speaking Data Implementation Consultant — €31,000-€51,000
Sr. Professional Services Engineer Consultant — expected salary range $145,400-$175,000 (San Francisco/Raleigh)
Dissemination & Implementation Specialist (Traumatic Brain Injury) — likely salary range $124,093-$129,766

seniority_distribution_notes: The bulk of the sample (128 of 202, ~63%) is mid-level/unspecified individual-contributor titles (\"Consultant,\" \"Implementation Consultant,\" \"Technical Consultant\") with no seniority qualifier. A meaningful senior slice (45, ~22%) carries \"Senior/Sr./Advanced/Strategic\" in the title, and a further 25 (~12%) are Principal/Chief/Director/Manager/Lead titles — this is a real senior-heavy tail, consistent with the brief's technical-track ladder (Consulting Engineer -> Senior -> Staff/Principal -> Practice Lead). Genuinely junior/entry titles (Junior, Associate, Recent Graduate, Student) are rare — only 4 of 202 — confirming this archetype is overwhelmingly a lateral-hire/experienced-hire role rather than a new-grad pipeline in the live postings, even though the brief correctly notes Deloitte Digital-style new-grad consultancy programs exist as an entry path elsewhere in the market (those specific postings didn't surface in this Greenhouse/Lever/Ashby/Workday-sourced corpus, which skews toward product-company PS orgs and SI delivery roles rather than campus recruiting).
notable_divergence_from_existing_brief: Mostly confirms the brief, with three nuances worth flagging. (1) Travel: the brief frames historical consultancy travel as "4-5 days/week" pre-COVID, shifting to biweekly hybrid. The fresh corpus's 28 postings with an explicit travel percentage cluster at 10-30% (most common), with only federal/public-sector-cleared roles (HPE, Reply SAP) stating 50-80% — this corroborates the brief's "travel load is lighter/lower now" narrative more strongly than the historical baseline, and suggests the archetype's current travel_embed_willingness target (3, weight 0.8) may be slightly high relative to what most live postings actually state, though the weight is defensible given the real variance (10% to 80%) and the federal-role outliers. (2) Utilization as \"the dominant KPI\": the literal word \"utilization\" appears in only 5 of 202 posting texts — postings talk far more often in terms of go-live dates, on-time/on-budget delivery (20 postings), and customer satisfaction/KPIs generically (14) than they name billable-utilization percentages directly. This doesn't contradict the brief (utilization is an internal PS-org metric less likely to appear in an external-facing JD than in internal compensation literature), but it means the comp-structure and outcome_accountability sections of the brief lean on off-posting industry literature more than on this posting corpus itself — worth flagging so a reviewer doesn't assume utilization language is directly JD-sourced. (3) The brief's new-grad/associate consultancy entry path (Deloitte Digital, etc.) is essentially invisible in this specific corpus (4 of 202 titles), which is a corpus-composition artifact (this pull skews toward product-company PS/Implementation orgs and enterprise SI delivery-consultant reqs, not campus recruiting pages) rather than a real market shift, and should not be read as contradicting that part of the brief.

dimension_evidence.note: Postings consistently show SOW/engagement-scoped work with named milestones and go-live targets, but require judgment within scope (architecture decisions, scope-creep management implied by "on-time/on-budget" language in 20 postings) — moderate, not high or low, ambiguity is well supported.

Postings describe structured workshop/milestone cadences (kickoff, discovery, delivery, go-live) rather than an inbound-ticket queue, consistent with moderate rather than high interrupt tolerance; the corpus doesn't directly discuss escalation-driven interruptions, so this is inferred/consistent rather than directly confirmed.

No postings in the corpus mention on-call, paging, or incident-response duties — success language is entirely delivery/schedule/CSAT-framed (KPI in 14 postings, SLA in only 4, mostly unrelated to on-call). Low target is well supported.

Integration work (119/202) and debugging integration issues during rollout are a recurring theme, but framed as scoped delivery work with research time rather than live-SLA firefighting — moderate depth is supported.

"Architect" appears in many titles (Technical Architect, Solutions Architect, Data & AI Platform Architect) and postings describe guiding architecture decisions, but consistently scoped to one client's environment/SOW rather than product-wide scale — moderate target supported.

Skill lists span multiple adjacent technologies per posting (SQL, APIs, cloud platforms, SAP/Salesforce/ERP module-specific stacks) rather than single deep specialization or fully generalist breadth — supports the "2-3 adjacent layers" framing.

20 postings explicitly reference on-time/on-budget delivery and 14 reference KPIs, supporting real but bounded-to-engagement accountability; however the literal word "utilization" (the brief's core evidence for this dimension) appears in only 5 of 202 posting texts, so this dimension's target is better supported by delivery/schedule language in the corpus than by utilization language specifically — worth noting for a reviewer relying on posting text alone.

Strongly supported: communication skills (72/202), client-facing (24/202), presentation skills (18/202), and "trusted advisor"/workshop-leading language recur constantly across the sample — this is one of the best-evidenced dimensions in the corpus.

"Training" appears in 100/202 postings, higher-frequency than the target might suggest at first glance, but nearly all instances are client-specific training delivery (classroom sessions, train-the-trainer, delivery kits) embedded in an engagement rather than public-facing teaching/content creation — consistent with a low-moderate rather than high target.

Well supported: 19 postings explicitly require managing/juggling multiple concurrent client engagements (Okta uses "lead multiple concurrent customer deployment projects" twice; Samsara uses "juggle multiple customer engagements in parallel" across 7 of its 9 postings) — matches the brief's v1.1 citation of this exact pattern.

Corpus confirms defined-engagement framing (kickoff through go-live/handoff language, 14 postings using "go-live" explicitly) consistent with time-boxed relationships rather than durable account ownership, matching the brief's prior JD-research finding.

Comp signals confirm base+bonus/variable structure broadly (Axon, Samsara, Cisco, GitLab all state bonus/incentive on top of base explicitly), but only a minority of postings quantify the incentive mix, and none in this corpus state a precise 70/30 base/incentive split — the specific ratio is brief-literature-sourced, not directly confirmed by posting text, though the qualitative direction (real variable comp layered on base) is well supported.

Custom scripting/configuration and integration work is a constant theme (Python 42/202, JavaScript 20/202, SQL 34/202), but always framed as delivering a configured client solution rather than product code shipped to a broad user base — moderate target supported.

28 postings state an explicit travel percentage ranging 10-80%, clustering at 25-30% for most enterprise SaaS PS roles and spiking to 50-80% only for federal/cleared roles (HPE, some Reply SAP reqs) — real and variable, but current live postings skew toward the lower/moderate end more than the brief's "4-5 days/week" historical framing, so the target/weight are directionally supported but the corpus suggests actual current travel intensity is more moderate than that historical anchor implies.

No postings in the corpus (including security-adjacent ones like Saviynt IAM/PAM and Tanium) frame the work as adversarial/red-team threat modeling — it's configuration and access-governance delivery, not offensive security. Low target supported.

No postings reference hardware/physical resource constraints as a core skill; even hardware-adjacent postings (HPE HPC Technical Consultant) frame the work as software configuration and customer enablement around the hardware, not physical-constraint engineering itself. Low target supported.

A minority of titles (25/202) are Manager/Director/Lead-level, consistent with the brief's bifurcated ladder, but the bulk of the corpus (163/202) is IC-level without formal reports, and even senior ICs (Principal Technical Consultant) are evaluated on delivery/engagement metrics rather than headcount management. Low-moderate target supported.

Training/documentation deliverables in the corpus are consistently client-specific and private (delivery kits, runbooks, classroom training for one customer's staff), not public conference talks or published content — low target supported.

AI/LLM terms appear in roughly half the postings (103/202), but nearly all are generic company-culture boilerplate ("we embrace AI as a core productivity multiplier") or product-category context (e.g. Databricks AI Platform Architect selling an AI product) rather than a requirement to build/train ML models — low target for ML engineering fluency specifically remains well supported.
No postings in the sample name iOS/Android/mobile-platform skills as a requirement. Low target supported.

SQL and data-migration work appear regularly (34/202 SQL, ERP/data-focused postings like Hudl's "Implementation Consultant, Data"), but this is applied data-migration/configuration work within an implementation, not building/operating data-infrastructure platforms — low target is defensible though this is the dimension where the evidence is least clean; a reviewer might consider whether the target is slightly conservative given how often data-migration work appears.

AWS (41), Azure (34), GCP (17), and Kubernetes (20) all appear as secondary/supporting skills across a meaningful minority of postings, consistent with "real but secondary" — target and weight both supported.
